title: Browser Presigning, POST Policy, and CORS · Compiler Case: Direct Upload Finalization
summary: Predict unknown input, typed state, command, stream, promise, storage, cache, security, cost, and recovery outcomes before reviewing least-privilege browser upload session; use server-created intents, exact signed headers, browser runtime constraints, CORS separation, and idempotent finalization. Planned artifact: Add and verify the direct upload finalization increment in least-privilege browser upload session. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal…
objectives: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, finalize by matching intent to HEAD or event evidence, checksum, version, size, owner, scan result, and idempotent state transition.
In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, configure the smallest origin, method, header, exposure, and age CORS rules while keeping IAM and bucket policy as authorization.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Predict Direct Upload Finalization · Theory Direct Upload Finalizati…
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 3295 during a multi-tenant authorization audit. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to finalize a browser upload intent while separating CORS from IAM and promise completion; keep AbortSignal and stream cleanup under one named owner; then double concurrent Upload instances and preserve aggregate memory and worker budgets. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Direct Upload Finalization contract for the supplied data.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, finalize by matching intent to HEAD or event evidence, checksum, version, size, owner, scan result, and idempotent state transition.

hints: For S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Defend the Direct Upload Finalization model · Theory Direct Upload F…, name the stakeholder, unknown schema, file, object version, authorization, byte, time, promise, stream, cache, cost, abort, cleanup, and recovery boundary, Direct Upload Finalization precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Defend the Direct Upload Finalization model · Theory Direct Upload F…, trace this concrete input and predict runtime validation, authorization, custody, integrity, range, stream, cache, retry, cost, cleanup, and recovery evidence before reviewing it: A theory TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 7547 during a Node stream backpressure incident. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to finalize a browser upload intent while separating CORS from IAM and promise completion; keep AbortSignal and stream cleanup under one named owner; then deliver a duplicate or reordered event and prevent duplicate stakeholder effect. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, finalize by matching intent to HEAD or event evidence, checksum, version, size, owner, scan result, and idempotent state transition.

For S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Defend the Direct Upload Finalization model · Theory Direct Upload F…, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, file, object, policy, transfer, integrity, cache, fault, cost, restore, and controlled AWS transfer evidence: The browser success event alone proves the expected object is complete and belongs to the session. Type annotations, generated SDK shapes, and resolved promises do not strengthen that runtime claim.
